title_en: Methodology — Evaluating Ibn Majah's Unique Narrations
title_id: Metodologi — Mengevaluasi Riwayat Unik Ibn Majah
content_en:
As-Suyuti's approach to the commentary on Sunan Ibn Majah is shaped by the particular challenges posed by the collection. Because Ibn Majah included narrations of varying quality — from sahih to mawdu' (fabricated) — the commentator must serve as both explicator and critic, helping the reader understand what the hadiths mean while also guiding them in assessing what can reliably be attributed to the Prophet, peace be upon him.

As-Suyuti's primary critical tool in this commentary is the concept of zawa'id — the additional hadiths in Ibn Majah not found in the other five canonical collections. By identifying these unique hadiths and subjecting them to chain-based analysis, as-Suyuti provides the reader with the information needed to use them appropriately. For hadiths found in all six collections or in the other five, the reader can consult the more thoroughly authenticated versions. For hadiths unique to Ibn Majah, as-Suyuti's grading becomes particularly important.

His grading system follows the standard hadith terminology: sahih (rigorously authenticated), hasan (good), da'if (weak), and mawdu' (fabricated). For each grading, he provides the basis of his assessment by referring to the conditions of the narrators in the chain. He draws on the same biographical sources used throughout the hadith commentary tradition — al-Mizzi's Tahdhib al-Kamal, Ibn Hajar's Tahdhib al-Tahdhib, and adh-Dhahabi's Mizan al-I'tidal.

As-Suyuti also engages with Ibn Majah's chapter headings, which often encode a legal ruling or indicate the position Ibn Majah himself derived from the hadith. This feature of the Sunan connects it to the broader tradition of using hadith collections as vehicles for legal methodology, and as-Suyuti's commentary makes this connection explicit.

For hadiths that are weak but have supporting narrations, as-Suyuti notes the supporting evidence, which may elevate the hadith to the level of hasan li-ghayrihi (good by virtue of external support). This nuanced approach prevents both the blanket rejection of all weak narrations in Ibn Majah and their uncritical acceptance.
content_id:
Pendekatan al-Suyuti dalam syarah atas Sunan Ibn Majah dibentuk oleh tantangan-tantangan khusus yang ditimbulkan oleh koleksi ini. Karena Ibn Majah memasukkan riwayat-riwayat dengan kualitas yang bervariasi — dari sahih hingga mawdu' (palsu) — komentator harus berperan sekaligus sebagai penjelas dan kritikus, membantu pembaca memahami apa yang dimaksudkan hadis-hadis tersebut sekaligus membimbing mereka dalam menilai apa yang dapat diandalkan sebagai atribusi kepada Nabi, shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam.

Alat kritis utama al-Suyuti dalam syarah ini adalah konsep zawa'id — hadis-hadis tambahan dalam Ibn Majah yang tidak terdapat dalam lima koleksi kanonik lainnya. Dengan mengidentifikasi hadis-hadis unik ini dan mengkaji sanadnya, al-Suyuti memberikan kepada pembaca informasi yang diperlukan untuk menggunakannya secara tepat. Untuk hadis-hadis yang terdapat dalam keenam koleksi atau dalam lima lainnya, pembaca dapat merujuk pada versi yang lebih thoroughly diautentikasi. Untuk hadis-hadis yang unik bagi Ibn Majah, penilaian al-Suyuti menjadi sangat penting.

Sistem penilaiannya mengikuti terminologi hadis standar: sahih (diautentikasi secara ketat), hasan (baik), da'if (lemah), dan mawdu' (palsu). Untuk setiap penilaian, ia memberikan dasar asesmen dengan merujuk pada kondisi para perawi dalam sanad. Ia mengandalkan sumber-sumber biografi yang sama yang digunakan sepanjang tradisi syarah hadis — Tahdhib al-Kamal karya al-Mizzi, Tahdhib al-Tahdhib karya Ibn Hajar, dan Mizan al-I'tidal karya al-Dzahabi.

Al-Suyuti juga berinteraksi dengan judul-judul bab Ibn Majah, yang sering kali mengandung kode sebuah ketetapan hukum atau mengindikasikan posisi yang Ibn Majah sendiri simpulkan dari hadis tersebut. Fitur Sunan ini menghubungkannya dengan tradisi yang lebih luas dalam menggunakan koleksi hadis sebagai wahana metodologi hukum, dan syarah al-Suyuti membuat hubungan ini menjadi eksplisit.

Untuk hadis-hadis yang lemah tetapi memiliki riwayat-riwayat pendukung, al-Suyuti mencatat bukti pendukung tersebut, yang dapat mengangkat hadis ke tingkat hasan li-ghayrihi (baik karena dukungan eksternal). Pendekatan yang bernuansa ini mencegah baik penolakan menyeluruh terhadap semua riwayat lemah dalam Ibn Majah maupun penerimaan tanpa kritik terhadap riwayat-riwayat tersebut.
content_ar: As-Suyuti's approach to the commentary on Sunan ابن ماجه is shaped by the particular challenges posed by the collection. لأن ابن ماجه included narrations of varying quality — from صحيح to موضوع' (fabricated) — the commentator must serve as both explicator and critic, helping the reader understand what the hadiths mean بينما أيضاً guiding them in assessing what can reliably be attributed to النبي ﷺ, ﷺ. As-Suyuti's الأساسي البالغ الأهمية tool in this commentary is the concept of zawa'id — the additional hadiths in ابن ماجه not found in the other five canonical collections. By identifying these unique hadiths and subjecting them to chain-based analysis, as-Suyuti provides the reader with the information needed to use them appropriately. For hadiths found in all six collections or in the other five, the reader can consult the more thoroughly authenticated versions. For hadiths unique to ابن ماجه, as-Suyuti's grading becomes particularly المهم. His grading system follows the standard الحديث terminology: صحيح (rigorously authenticated), حسن (الخير), ضعيف (weak), and موضوع' (fabricated). For each grading, he provides the basis of his assessment by referring to the conditions of the narrators in the chain. He draws on the same biographical sources used throughout the الحديث commentary التراث — al-Mizzi's Tahdhib al-Kamal, Ibn Hajar's Tahdhib al-Tahdhib, and adh-Dhahabi's الميزان al-I'tidal. As-Suyuti أيضاً engages with ابن ماجه's الباب headings, which often encode a legal ruling or indicate the position ابن ماجه himself derived from the الحديث. This feature of the Sunan connects it to the broader التراث of using الحديث collections as vehicles for legal methodology, and as-Suyuti's commentary makes this connection explicit. For hadiths that are weak but have supporting narrations, as-Suyuti notes the supporting evidence, which may elevate the الحديث to the level of حسن li-ghayrihi (الخير by الفضيلة of external support). This nuanced approach prevents both the blanket rejection of all weak narrations in ابن ماجه and their uncritical acceptance.
